items.title: Presence Is Reality: Rethinking Virtual and Real-World Consciousness
items.author: Oliver Singleton, Aikaterini FotopoulouClinical, Educational and Health Psychology Research Department, Division of Psychology and Language Sciences, University College London
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. The sense of presence is typically defined as the feeling of “being there” in a virtual environment, whereas the sense of reality is defined as the ability to discriminate between real and unreal phenomena. items.title: Socioeconomic Status Disparities in Children’s Cognition—Differences in Degree or Kind?
items.author: Lingyan Hu, Martha J. Farah
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. The “socioeconomic achievement gap” refers to socioeconomic disparities in children’s academic outcomes. items.title: Power, Privilege, and Positionality in Psychology Departments: Toward a Framework for Inclusive Excellence
items.author: Leher SinghDepartment of Psychology, University of Maryland, Baltimore County
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. items.title: Revisiting Hebb: The Mechanisms of Repetition Learning
items.author: Philipp Musfeld, Klaus Oberauer
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. In 1961, Donald Hebb established a classic paradigm for studying repetition learning: He asked participants to remember several memory sets for an immediate serial recall task and repeated one set multiple times throughout the experiment. Participants’ ...</article>

items.title: Artificial Intelligence and the Psychology of Human Connection
items.author: Ryan L. Boyd, David M. Markowitz
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. As artificial intelligence (AI) becomes increasingly embedded in social life, understanding its interpersonal and psychological implications is urgent yet undertheorized. This article introduces the machine-integrated relational adaptation (MIRA) model, a ...</article>

items.title: How U.S. Funding Restrictions Endanger the Future of Psychology: Consequences for Research, Training, and Clinical Care
items.author: Kaitlyn A. Kaiser
items.content: <article>Perspectives on Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. In 2025, U.S. policy changes imposed sweeping limits on the scope of research eligible for federal support alongside sharp reductions to science-agency budgets, threatening the foundations of psychological science.
